query: example of dystrophic calcification in sarcoidosis vs other conditions like hyperparathyroidism or hypervitaminosis D

retrieved_docs.title: Vitamin D Toxicity -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Vitamin D hypervitaminosis should be differentiated from other causes leading to hypercalcemia.  Other diseases that mimic the signs and symptoms of hypervitaminosis are as follows: Hypercalcemia of malignancy Hypercalcemia of granulomatous diseases Primary, secondary, and tertiary hyperparathyroidism Vitamin A toxicity Thyrotoxicosis Paget disease Hypercalcemia caused by prolonged immobilization Milk-alkali syndrome

retrieved_docs.title: Calcitriol -- Contraindications -- Warning and Precautions
retrieved_docs.content: Lymphomas or certain granulomatous diseases like sarcoidosis may activate extrarenal 1α-hydroxylase, the enzyme responsible for converting 25-hydroxyvitamin D to calcitriol. This leads to elevated calcitriol levels and hypercalcemia of malignancy, which should be separated from iatrogenic administration. [28]

retrieved_docs.title: Malignancy-Related Hypercalcemia -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: The initial evaluation of hypercalcemia requires a comprehensive history and physical examination, which can help identify the underlying cause and pathology. Prior laboratory data are valuable, providing insights into baseline calcium levels and the duration of hypercalcemia. Medication history, including prescription drugs, over-the-counter vitamins, and supplements, along with dietary history, family history, and any history of granulomatous disease, should be systematically reviewed. Initial labs should include PTH levels, as this helps differentiate between PTH-related hypercalcemia and non-PTH–mediated hypercalcemia. PTH-related hypercalcemia is seen in conditions such as PHPT and familial hyperparathyroid syndromes, while non-PTH–related hypercalcemia occurs in malignancies, granulomatous diseases, endocrine disorders, and vitamin D intoxication. [19]

retrieved_docs.title: Pseudohypoparathyroidism -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Vitamin D deficiency This condition presents with hypocalcemia and elevated PTH levels (secondary hyperparathyroidism) but obviously low 25 OH vitamin D levels.

retrieved_docs.title: Hypercalciuria -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Hypercalciuria may be caused by disease states not discussed above. Some of these disorders are sarcoidosis, PTH-related peptide production, malignancy, non-calcium based kidney stones (such those composed of uric acid or cystine), or exogenous vitamin D consumption.

retrieved_docs.title: Nephrocalcinosis -- Etiology -- 1. Conditions causing both hypercalcemia and hypercalciuria
retrieved_docs.content: Primary hyperparathyroidism Vitamin D therapy Sarcoidosis Milk-alkali syndrome Congenital hypothyroidism

retrieved_docs.title: Malignancy-Related Hypercalcemia -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: The following conditions should be considered when evaluating hypercalcemia: Adrenal insufficiency Berylliosis Coccidioidomycosis Crohn disease Hyperkalemia Hypermagnesemia Hypernatremia Hyperparathyroidism Hyperphosphatemia Hyperthyroidism Milk-alkali syndrome Vitamin D toxicity

retrieved_docs.title: Rickets -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Conditions which cause hypocalcemia: Primary hypoparathyroidism

retrieved_docs.title: Calcitriol -- Contraindications -- Warning and Precautions
retrieved_docs.content: Relative contraindications to calcitriol include patients with increased sensitivity to calcium dysregulation, including arteriosclerosis, cardiac disease, hyperphosphatemia, renal failure, and sarcoidosis. Patients with cardiac disease, especially those taking digoxin, are at an increased risk for cardiac arrhythmias from hypercalcemia and should be dosed conservatively. [26] Patients with renal failure are susceptible to increased risk for vitamin D-induced hypercalcemia, and chronic hypercalcemia may lead to soft tissue calcification, nephrocalcinosis, and other toxicities.

retrieved_docs.title: Hungry Bone Syndrome -- Pertinent Studies and Ongoing Trials -- Vitamin D Supplementation
retrieved_docs.content: Vitamin D supplementation, whether with cholecalciferol or calcitriol, can help reduce the severity of the hyperparathyroid state in secondary hyperparathyroidism, particularly in the setting of end-stage renal disease. In patients with hypercalcemia awaiting parathyroidectomy for hyperparathyroidism, there may be concern and anxiety about administering vitamin D replacement due to fears of exacerbating the hypercalcemic state.

retrieved_docs.title: Osteopenia -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Homocystinuria Hyperparathyroidism Imaging in osteomalacia Mastocytosis Multiple myeloma Paget disease Scurvy Sickle cell anaemia

retrieved_docs.title: Hypercalcemia -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: The etiology of hypercalcemia can be divided into 2 major categories: parathyroid hormone–mediated and non-parathyroid hormone–mediated.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Fat Soluble Vitamins -- Testing -- Vitamin D
retrieved_docs.content: Vitamin D is measurable in the serum in 25-hydroxyvitamin D and 1,25-dihydroxy vitamin D. 25-hydroxyvitamin D is the principal circulating form (levels in ng/ml). It has a half-life of 2 weeks and is the best measure of vitamin D status. Testing is indicated in populations at high risk for fractures, including those with osteoporosis, osteopenia, and the elderly. Treatment initiation depends on the extent of the deficiency. The Endocrine Society states that levels of 25-hydroxyvitamin D below 20 ng/ml are considered insufficient. According to The US Institute of Medicine, a 25-hydroxyvitamin D level of 20 ng/mL or greater is optimal for bone strength. Levels exceeding 100 ng/ml are considered toxic and put the patient at risk for hypercalcemia, calculi, and renal damage. Hence, optimum levels appear to be between 20 to 30 ng/ml, with levels over 50 ng/ml avoided. Testing levels of 1,25-hydroxyvitamin D may also be indicated in patients with kidney failure or suspected hypercalcemia from granulomas in sarcoidosis or suspected hyperparathyroidism. Low levels of the active metabolite are commonly observed in early renal failure, while increased levels may present in sarcoidosis and primary hyperparathyroidism. The RDA for vitamin D is 600 IU per day. In individuals older than 70 years, the RDA is 800 IU per day.

retrieved_docs.title: Calcitriol -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: Hypercalcemia, vitamin D toxicity, and hypersensitivity to calcitriol or any vitamin D analog are contraindications to taking systemic calcitriol.

retrieved_docs.title: Rickets -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Renal insufficiency: Bone disease, also known as renal osteodystrophy, occurs due to many reasons such as the reduced formation of 1,25- dihydroxy vitamin D, metabolic acidosis, retention of phosphate, aluminum toxicity, and secondary hyperparathyroidism.

retrieved_docs.title: Vitamin D Deficiency -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: End-organ resistance: Hereditary vitamin D-resistant rickets may cause end-organ resistance to this nutrient. [15]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2030
retrieved_docs.content: Vitamin D and analogues is a pharmacological class that encompasses both natural vitamin D2 (ergocalciferol) and synthetic compounds such as dihydrotachysterol; dihydrotachysterol is identified as a member of the Vitamin D and analogues group, and vitamin D2 is likewise included within this broader category. This classification highlights the relationship between the natural form of vitamin D2 and its analogues, informing clinicians about the spectrum of agents that share similar biological activity and can be considered when selecting vitamin D–related therapies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 3 -- Community 2179
retrieved_docs.content: Kalcitriol and dihydrotachysterol are both classified as members of the “Vitamin D and analogues” group, indicating that they are vitamin D–related compounds that share similar biological activity in regulating calcium and phosphate metabolism. This classification highlights their common therapeutic role as vitamin D analogues used to treat conditions such as hypocalcemia, secondary hyperparathyroidism, and certain bone disorders.

Cholekalciferol (vitamin D₃) is a steroid hormone that belongs to the broader vitamin D and analogues class and to the category “hormones, their metabolites, and precursors.” It functions as a calcium‑regulating hormone that promotes absorption of fluid and electrolytes in the small intestine, supports mineral balance and bone health, and decreases renal calcium (Ca²⁺) excretion, reflecting its role in calcium homeostasis. Cholekalciferol is linked to the disposition of the Hormone entity, may be formulated with salicylic acid (18 mg/mL) as an inactive ingredient, and can be used therapeutically in conditions such as ductal stenosis. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1498
retrieved_docs.content: Oxandrolone, an anabolic androgenic steroid, is contraindicated in patients with hypercalcemia, a disorder marked by elevated serum calcium levels; administering oxandrolone in the setting of hypercalcemia can worsen calcium metabolism disturbances and increase the risk of related complications, so clinicians should avoid prescribing this medication to individuals who have this electrolyte abnormality.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 408
retrieved_docs.content: Vitamin D and analogues represent a therapeutic class that includes dihydrotachysterol as one of its members. Both the Vitamin D and analogues group and the plain form of Vitamin A are classified under the broader category “vitamin A and D, incl. combinations of the two,” which groups together individual vitamins A and D as well as their combined formulations. This hierarchical organization highlights that dihydrotachysterol is a specific analogue within the Vitamin D family, while Vitamin A, plain, and the entire Vitamin D and analogues class share a common higher‑level classification that is relevant for understanding their nutritional roles, potential combined use, and therapeutic management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 3 -- Community 2056
retrieved_docs.content: Steroid hormone encompasses kalcitriol as a subordinate entity, indicating that kalcitriol is a specific steroid hormone derivative. Both kalcitriol and dihydrotachysterol are classified as members of the group Vitamin D and analogues, reflecting their shared chemical nature as vitamin D–related compounds. The category Vitamin D and analogues, together with Vitamin A, plain, are each identified as belonging to the broader classification “vitamin A and D, incl. combinations of the two,” which groups individual vitamins and their analogues that involve either vitamin A, vitamin D, or combined formulations. This hierarchy highlights the relationship of certain steroid‑derived vitamin D analogues to the larger vitamin A/ D family, aiding clinicians in understanding how these agents are organized within pharmacologic and nutritional contexts.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 3 -- Community 2041
retrieved_docs.content: Vitamin K 2 and vitamin K 3 are both classified as vitamins. As members of the vitamin group, they are directly related to نقص التغذية (nutritional deficiency), highlighting that insufficient intake or absorption of these vitamins can contribute to a state of nutritional deficiency. Recognizing vitamin K 2 and vitamin K 3 within the broader context of vitamins is essential for identifying and preventing nutrient‑related health problems.
